tt_insn_vec.help: Portion of vector registers that is actually used. Available only for instructions belonging to a SIMD/vector instructions set (like x86 AVX)

tt_Recip_throughput.help: Average number of cycles to retire that instruction if repeated (assuming independent instructions, with no read-after-write dependency)

hint.details:
Calling (and then returning from) a function prevents many compiler optimizations (like vectorization), breaks control flow (which reduces pipeline performance) and executes extra instructions to save/restore the registers used inside it, which is very expensive (dozens of cycles). Consider to inline small functions.
 - randlc(double*, double): 1 occurrences<<list_path_1_call_1>>
 - rcm_ordering_from_adjacency(int, std::vector<int, std::allocator<int> > const&, std::vector<int, std::allocator<int> > const&, std::vector<int, std::allocator<int> >&, std::vector<int, std::allocator<int> >&): 1 occurrences<<list_path_1_call_2>>
 - std::vector<double, std::allocator<double> >::_M_default_append(unsigned long): 1 occurrences<<list_path_1_call_3>>
 - std::vector<int, std::allocator<int> >::_M_fill_assign(unsigned long, int const&): 1 occurrences<<list_path_1_call_4>>
 - std::vector<int, std::allocator<int> >::resize(unsigned long): 3 occurrences<<list_path_1_call_5>>
 - std::vector<int, std::allocator<int> >::vector(unsigned long, int const&, std::allocator<int> const&): 2 occurrences<<list_path_1_call_6>>
 - std::vector<int, std::allocator<int> >::vector(unsigned long, std::allocator<int> const&): 1 occurrences<<list_path_1_call_7>>
 - std::vector<int, std::allocator<int> >::~vector(): 4 occurrences<<list_path_1_call_8>>
 - timer_clear(int): 3 occurrences<<list_path_1_call_9>>
 - timer_start(int): 1 occurrences<<list_path_1_call_10>>
 - unknown: 17 occurrences<<list_path_1_call_11>>
hint.title: CALL instructions

hint.details:
These instructions generate more than one micro-operation and only one of them can be decoded during a cycle and the extra micro-operations increase pressure on execution units.
 - PUSH: 1 occurrences<<list_path_1_complex_1>>
 - SUB: 1 occurrences<<list_path_1_complex_2>>
 - VCVTSI2SD: 1 occurrences<<list_path_1_complex_3>>
 - VPINSRQ: 3 occurrences<<list_path_1_complex_4>>
 - VZEROUPPER: 7 occurrences<<list_path_1_complex_5>>
hint.title: Complex instructions

hint.workaround: Avoid mixing data with different types. In particular, check if the type of constants is the same as array elements.
hint.details:
- CLTQ: 3 occurrences<<list_path_1_cvt_1>>
 - VCVTSI2SD (INT32/64 to FP64, scalar): 1 occurrences<<list_path_1_cvt_2>>

expert.help: Compare the CQA-computed load/store/compute metrics with the capacity of the core. For instance if CQA computes 24 bytes in the loop in 2 cycles (that is 12 cycles per cycle) and if the core can process up to 16 bytes per cycle, that metric is 75%.
expert.title: Cycles and memory resources usage
expert.txt:
Assuming all data fit into the L1 cache, each call to the function takes 153.25 cycles. At this rate:
 - 6% of peak load performance is reached (4.41 out of 64.00 bytes loaded per cycle (GB/s @ 1GHz))
 - 18% of peak store performance is reached (5.92 out of 32.00 bytes stored per cycle (GB/s @ 1GHz))

expert.title: Front-end bottlenecks
expert.txt: Performance is limited by instruction throughput (loading/decoding program instructions to execution core) (front-end is a bottleneck).

header:
Warnings:
 - The number of fused uops of the instruction [ENDBR64] is unknown
 - Detected a function call instruction: ignoring called function instructions.
Rerun with --follow-calls=append to include them to analysis  or with --follow-calls=inline to simulate inlining.
0% of peak computational performance is used (0.00 out of 32.00 FLOP per cycle (GFLOPS @ 1GHz))

gain.workaround:
- Try to reorganize arrays of structures to structures of arrays
 - Consider to permute loops (see vectorization gain report)
gain.title: Code clean check
gain.txt:
Detected a slowdown caused by scalar integer instructions (typically used for address computation).
By removing them, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 153.25 to 34.00 cycles (4.51x speedup).

gain.workaround:
- Try another compiler or update/tune your current one
 - Make array accesses unit-stride:
  * If your function streams arrays of structures (AoS), try to use structures of arrays instead (SoA)
gain.details:
18% of SSE/AVX instructions are used in vector version (process two or more data elements in vector registers):
 - 23% of SSE/AVX loads are used in vector version.
 - 13% of SSE/AVX stores are used in vector version.
 - 24% of SSE/AVX instructions that are not load, store, addition, subtraction nor multiply instructions are used in vector version.
Since your execution units are vector units, only a fully vectorized function can use their full power.
gain.title: Vectorization
gain.txt:
Your function is not vectorized.
Only 26% of vector register length is used (average across all SSE/AVX instructions).
By fully vectorizing your function, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 153.25 to 33.62 cycles (4.56x speedup).

gain.title: Execution units bottlenecks
gain.txt: Found no such bottlenecks but see expert reports for more complex bottlenecks.
